questions.question: According to Bernoulli's theorem, as velocity increases static pressure:
questions.explanation: Total energy remains constant, so as the speed of the fluid increases, the static pressure must decrease to conserve energy.

questions.question: You experience a high frequency vibration during cruise flight, the most likely cause is:
options: tail rotor is out of balance
main rotor is out of balance

questions.question: Ground resonance is most likely caused by:
options: a semi rigid main rotor being out of balance
a geometric imbalance in a fully articulated rotor head
questions.explanation: Ground resonance is a dynamic instability that can occur in helicopters when the main rotor system interacts with the landing gear during ground operations. It is most commonly caused by a geometric imbalance in a fully articulated rotor head.

questions.question: Which flight condition is most likely to encounter vortex ring state?
options: on a steep descent with high power
questions.kd: Unit 1.3.3.  2.2.3(a) vortex ring state (settling with power)

questions.question: Which flight condition is most likely to encounter retreating blade stall?
options: on a steep descent with high power
questions.explanation: The flight condition most likely to encounter retreating blade stall is high forward airspeed combined with high power (high angle of attack). At excessive speeds or high power settings, the angle of attack on the retreating blade can become too high, leading to a stall on the retreating side of the rotor disc, causing vibrations, the nose to pitch up and roll towards the retreating side.

questions.question: Retreating blade stall is more likely in which flight condition?
options: High speed and high density altitude
low speed and low density altitude
questions.explanation: Retreating blade stall is more likely to occur at high forward airspeeds and high power settings, which can be exacerbated by high density altitude conditions. High density altitude reduces the overall performance of the helicopter, requiring higher angles of attack to maintain lift, which increases the risk of retreating blade stall.

questions.question: In the event of an engine failure, which component disengages the engine from the rotor system?
questions.explanation: The freewheeling unit disengages the engine from the rotor system during an engine failure, enabling safe autorotation. The freewheel unit allows the rotor system to continue spinning freely during an autorotation, independent of engine power.

questions.question: What is disc solidity?
options: the strength of the main rotor blades
the ratio of total blade area to disc area
the length of the blades whilst under power
The stability of the rotors during autorotation
questions.explanation: Disc solidity is a measure of how much of the rotor disc area is actually covered by the rotor blades. Disc Solidity = (Total blade area) / (Rotor disc area)

questions.question: A fully articulated rotor system can:
options: flap up and down, lead and lag

questions.question: A delta-3 hinge:
options: connects the tail rotor drive shaft to the tail rotor gearbox
is an offset flapping hinge that reduces the amount of flap back
are found on fenestron tail rotors only
questions.explanation: The hinge is angled, so when a rotor blade flaps up or down, the delta-3 hinge causes an automatic pitch change that tends to reduce further flapping.

questions.question: During a takeoff in a helicopter, the pilot notices a marked improvement in performance and control effectiveness at approximately 16 to 24 knots of forward airspeed. This is primarily due to:
options: The retreating blade experiencing increased dynamic pressure
The helicopter entering Effective Translational Lift (ETL).
The stabilizer fin becoming fully effective.
questions.explanation: ETL occurs when the helicopter moves forward fast enough (typically around 16-24 knots) for the rotor system to move out of its own downwash and operate in clean, undisturbed air. This results in increased lift with the same power setting and improved rotor effiency.

questions.question: Which of the following is a primary symptom of retreating blade stall in forward flight?
options: Nose-down pitch and a sudden increase in RPM
Left roll and loss of tail rotor effectiveness
Nose-up pitch and violent right roll
Vibration and droop in rotor RPM
questions.explanation: A primary symptom of retreating blade stall in forward flight is a nose-up pitch and violent right roll. This occurs because the retreating blade is experiencing reduced lift, however the advanving blade is still providing lift but is felt 90 degrees later due to gyroscopic precession. Therefore the helicopter tends to pitch up and roll to the right.

questions.question: Which flight condition is most likely to lead to vortex ring state?
options: High rate of descent, low power, forward airspeed
High rate of descent, high power, low forward airspeed
Low rate of descent, high power, moderate airspeed
questions.explanation: The flight condition most likely to lead to vortex ring state is high rate of descent with low or insufficient forward airspeed while using high power. In vortex ring state, the helicopter descends into its own descending column of induced vortices, leading to loss of lift and uncommanded descent despite high power input
questions.kd: Unit 1.3.3.  2.2.3(a) vortex ring state (settling with power)

questions.question: Mast bumping can only occur:
options: on a semi rigid rotor head
on a fully articulated rotor system
when hovering at altitude with a tail wind component
questions.explanation: Mast bumping can only occur in semi rigid (teetering) rotor systems, such as those found on some two-bladed helicopters (e.g., Robinson R22, R44). In a teetering rotor system, the rotor hub is like a seesaw. In low G conditions, there is a risk that the rotor hub can contact ("bump") the mast, potentially leading to catastrophic failure.

questions.question: During an autorotation, the stalled region of the rotor disc is located:
options: at the inner region of the rotor disc closest to the rotor hub
at the mid point of the rotor blade
at the centre of pressure of the aerofoil
questions.explanation: During an autorotation, the stalled region of the rotor disc is located at the innermost (root) section of the rotor blades. In this region the airflow is insufficient to maintain lift; the blade is effectively stalled.

questions.question: What effect will an increase of density altitude have on the rate of descent during an autorotation?
questions.explanation: As density altitude increases, the rate of descent in autorotation increases. The reduced air density also decreases the overall lift produced by the rotor blades for a given blade pitch and rotor speed.

questions.question: Bernoulli's theorem states:
options: total pressure = static pressure  dynamic pressure
total pressure = 1/2 static pressure x dynamic pressure
total pressure = static pressure x 1/2 dynamic pressure
total pressure = static pressure x dynamic pressure
questions.explanation: Bernoulli's Theorem states in a steady, incompressible flow of an ideal fluid with no energy losses. As the velocity of a fluid (like air) increases, its static pressure decreases.
questions.kd: Unit 1.3.1.  2.2.1 Apply Bernoulli's theorem of constant energy flow to describe how an aerofoil produces lift, limited to the variation of kinetic energy (dynamic pressure) and potential energy (static pressure) as air flows through a venturi or over an aerofoil

questions.question: What is the force load factor on a helicopter in a 60° angle of bank?
questions.explanation:
At a 60° angle of bank, the load factor on a helicopter is 2 Gs. The load factor (G-force) in a banked turn is calculated using:
Load Factor
=
1
cos
⁡
(
bank angle
)
Load Factor= 
cos(bank angle)
1
​

questions.question: The angle of attack is:
options: The angle between the chord line and relative airflow
the angle between the induced flow and chord line
the angle between the introduced flow and the relative airflow
the angle between the rotational airflow and the chord line

questions.question: The Cl of the lift formula represents:
questions.explanation: Cl (Coefficient of Lift): A dimensionless number that quantifies how effectively a body (like an airfoil) generates lift at a given angle of attack and airfoil shape.
questions.kd: Unit 1.3.1   2.4.1(a) Cl and Cd - depend on shape and angle of attack of an aerofoil

questions.question: The ⍴ of the lift formula represents:
questions.explanation: In the lift formula, ⍴ (rho) represents air density.

questions.question: The S of the lift formula represents:
options: size, shape and surface of the aerofoil
questions.explanation: S is the surface area used to calculate lift, representing the size of the lifting surface. For helicopters, S is often the rotor disk area or the blade area considered.

questions.question: At what approximate airspeed would you experience best rate of climb in a typical small helicopter?
questions.explanation: In a typical small helicopter, the best rate of climb is usually achieved at an airspeed of approximately 50-60kt indicated airspeed. This speed range provides the optimal balance between low parasite drag (which increases at higher speeds) and reduced induced drag (which is high at low speeds).

questions.question: The V of the lift formula represents:
questions.explanation: V represents the true airspeed of the airflow over the wing or aerofoil.

questions.question: A semi rigid rotor system can:
options: flap up and down, lead and lag
questions.explanation: A semi rigid rotor system can flap up and down but does not have lead-lag hinges. The blades are rigidly attached to the hub, allowing them to flap but not to lead or lag independently. Some examples of helicopter types with semi rigid rotor heads include the Robinson R22 and R44, as well as the Bell 206.

questions.question: The Mean Chord line of an aerofoil is:
options: The line that is equidistant between the upper and lower sections of the aerofoil
the line that is equidistant between the trailing edge and leading edge of the aerofoil, from the rotor tip to the rotor hub
line that can vary with changes of angle of attack
a line drawn from the leading edge to the trailing edge
questions.explanation: The Mean Chord line of an aerofoil is the line drawn through the midpoints of all the chords of the aerofoil, effectively representing the average chord position along the span.

questions.question: As you hover from in ground effect to out of ground effect, what happens to the induced flow?
questions.explanation:
Induced flow is the downward airflow through the rotor disc generated to produce lift. In ground effect, the ground surface interferes with and slows down the downward airflow, requiring less induced flow to maintain hover.
As you move out of ground effect, this cushioning effect disappears and the rotor has to work harder to generate the same amount of lift, therefore more induced flow (stronger downward airflow) is required. This increases induced drag and power required.

questions.question: A helicopter is most vulnerable to LTE during:
options: Low altitude, low airspeed hover operations with high power settings
Descent at high airspeed in a nose-down attitude
Climb-out with a tailwind and low gross weight
Autorotation entry with minimal collective input
questions.explanation: A helicopter is most vulnerable to LTE (Loss of Tail Rotor Effectiveness) during low-speed, downwind or crosswind hover, especially when the wind is from the left or rear quarter. In certain wind directions and low airspeeds, tail rotor thrust becomes disrupted or ineffective due to main rotor vortices or turbulent air, reducing its ability to keep the helicopter from yawing.
questions.kd: Unit 1.3.3.  2.2.3(b) loss of tail rotor effectiveness (LTE)

questions.question: Which of the following is the primary aerodynamic cause of LTE?
options: Main rotor tip vortices interfering with tail rotor thrust
Overloading of the helicopter during high-altitude operations
Tail rotor operating in disturbed airflow from main rotor or wind direction
Sudden loss of hydraulic pressure to the anti-torque pedals
questions.kd: Unit 1.3.3.  2.2.3(b) loss of tail rotor effectiveness (LTE)

questions.question: Given a helicopter rotor diametre of 9m and is operating at a weight of 1256kg. What is the  disc loading?
What is the primary cause of dissymmetry of lift in a helicopter during forward flight?
options: Difference in airspeed between the advancing and retreating blades
Translational lift effects on retreating blades
questions.explanation: The primary cause of dissymmetry of lift in a helicopter during forward flight is the difference in relative airflow velocity between the advancing blade and the retreating blade of the main rotor.

questions.question: What aerodynamic principle causes dissymmetry of lift in forward flight?
options: Difference in airflow velocity across the rotor disc
Unequal blade angles due to feathering
questions.explanation: The difference in relative airflow velocity between the advancing and retreating rotor blades, which leads to unequal lift production on either side of the rotor disc.

questions.question: How does a fully articulated rotor system primarily manage dissymmetry of lift?
options: Through blade flapping and cyclic feathering
By increasing rotor RPM during forward flight

questions.question: Under which condition is the effect of dissymmetry of lift likely to be at its maximum?
options: During fast, low-altitude flight in calm air
During a vertical autorotation in turbulent conditions
During hover into wind with high collective
questions.explanation: Dissymmetry of lift occurs in helicopters because the advancing rotor blade moves faster relative to the air than the retreating blade, producing more lift on the advancing side, this effect increases with airspeed. At high forward speeds, the difference in relative airflow speed between advancing and retreating blades is greatest.

questions.question: If a pilot exceeds recommended forward airspeed limits and dissymmetry of lift is not properly managed, what is a likely consequence?
questions.explanation: If a pilot exceeds recommended forward airspeed limits and dissymmetry of lift is not properly managed, the likely consequence is the onset of retreating blade stall. As forward speed increases, the retreating blade experiences reduced relative airflow, which can lead to a stall condition on that side of the rotor disc, causing vibrations and control issues.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key strategy to prevent dynamic rollover?
options: Ensure both skids are clear before increasing collective
Use pedal input to counter roll
Lower the cyclic and maintain forward pitch

questions.question: What is the primary condition that makes dynamic rollover possible?
options: A pivot point exists and lift or thrust continues to increase
Tail rotor torque overcomes main rotor lift
Wind gusts exceed main rotor authority
Retreating blade stall causes roll input
questions.explanation: he primary condition that makes dynamic rollover possible is a pivot point combined with rolling motion and insufficient corrective input.

questions.question: The chord line of an aerofoil section is:
options: The line from the leading to the trailing edge which is equidistant from the upper and lower surfaces
the line which runs perpendicular to the centre of pressure
the line from the leading edge to the trailing edge
questions.explanation: The chord line of an aerofoil section is an imaginary straight line connecting the leading edge to the trailing edge of the aerofoil.
questions.kd: Unit 1.3.1   2.1.1(a) aerofoil span, chord, camber, thickness/chord ratio

questions.question: Blade sailing is a condition that can occur:
options: during start up and shut down
questions.explanation: Blade sailing is a condition that can occur when the rotor blades are allowed to rotate freely in very high winds while the rotor is not turning or is turning very slowly, such as when the helicopter is parked with the rotor unlocked. It happens when strong winds cause the rotor blades to flap and rotate on their hinges.

questions.question: The forward speed of a helicopter is limited to:
questions.explanation:
In forward flight, the rotor blades experience dissymmetry of lift:
The forward speed of a helicopter is limited primarily by retreating blade stall whereby the advancing blade (moving into the airflow) generates more lift and the retreating blade (moving away from airflow) generates less.
At high speeds, the retreating blade must work harder (via increased angle of attack) to produce equal lift. However, it comes to a point where the retreating blade can stall (angle of attack is too high)— losing lift and causing severe vibrations and control issues.

questions.question: Induced drag:
options: remains constant throughout all relative airspeeds
decreases with relative airspeed until best rate of climb speed, then increases
questions.explanation: Induced drag results from the production of lift and is most significant at low speeds and high angles of attack.
questions.kd: Unit 1.3.1   2.1.2 Explain the different types of drag and state the effect on total drag resulting from changes in IAS, aircraft weight and height, if any.

questions.question: Parasite Drag:
options: remains constant throughout all relative airspeeds
decreases with relative airspeed until best rate of climb speed, then increases
questions.explanation: Parasite drag on an aircraft is caused by non-lifting surfaces moving through the air, such as the fuselage, landing gear, antennas, and rotor hubs.
questions.kd: Unit 1.3.1   2.1.2 Explain the different types of drag and state the effect on total drag resulting from changes in IAS, aircraft weight and height, if any.

questions.question: The power required to overcome parasite drag:
options: is minimum at Mean Sea Level
is at minimum when at the best rate of climb speed
increases directly proportional with relative airspeed
questions.explanation: Parasite drag is caused by the helicopter's fuselage, landing gear, and other non-lifting surfaces moving through the air. In hover, the helicopter has no forward airspeed, so there's no significant parasite drag to overcome.
questions.kd: Unit 1.3.1   2.1.2 Explain the different types of drag and state the effect on total drag resulting from changes in IAS, aircraft weight and height, if any.

questions.question: An increase in the angle of attack of a rotor blade on a helicopter forward flight will cause the:
options: the centre of gravity to move aft
the centre of pressure to move aft
the separation point to move forward

questions.question: Ground effect is most effective when:
options: hovering over a hard level surface in nil wind
hovering over long grass in nil wind
questions.explanation: Ground effect occurs because the air cushion beneath the helicopter (or aircraft) reduces the induced drag by restricting the downward airflow. A hard, flat surface reflects the airflow smoothly, enhancing this cushion effect.

questions.question: With reference to the lift formula, Cl varies with:
questions.explanation: The coefficient of lift (Cₗ) varies primarily with the angle of attack (AOA) of the wing or aerofoil.
questions.kd: Unit 1.3.1   2.4.1(a) Cl and Cd - depend on shape and angle of attack of an aerofoil

questions.question: A NOTAR tail rotor system counter acts the main rotor torque by use of:
options: many small fins along the tail boom
questions.explanation: A NOTAR (No Tail Rotor) system counteracts the main rotor torque by using low-pressure air ducts to create a boundary layer of air along the tail boom. This creates a Coanda effect, which generates lateral thrust to counteract the torque produced by the main rotor.

questions.question: Dynamic rollover can occur due to:
options: an excessive rolling movement developing about the skid in contact with a sloping terrain
questions.explanation: Dynamic rollover can occur when a helicopter is on the ground and one skid becomes a pivot point while the helicopter rolls. If the rolling motion continues and corrective action is not taken, the helicopter can roll over onto its side or even upside down. This is often associated with operations on sloped terrain, but can also occur on flat surfaces if there is a strong crosswind or if the pilot applies excessive collective or pedal input.

questions.question: What flight configuration will achieve the greatest range in an autorotation?
options: low forward airspeed and high rotor RPM
low forward airspeed and low rotor RPM
high forward airspeed and high rotor RPM
high forward airspeed and low rotor RPM
questions.explanation: To achieve greatest range in autorotation, fly at the best glide speed specific to the helicopter model, maintain proper rotor RPM, and optimise pitch and attitude for efficient forward glide. The best airspeed that gives the maximum horizontal distance per unit of altitude lost.

questions.question: As the density altitude is increased, the Vne will:
options: only decrease in a teetering rotor system
questions.explanation: With increasing density altitude, Vne decreases to maintain safe operating margins under less favorable aerodynamic and performance conditions.

questions.question: The centre of pressure on an aerofoil is the point where:
options: all four forces are acting upon the helicopter
on the chordline of the aerofoil where the resultant lift force acts
are generally found at the leading edge
are generally found at the leading edge

questions.question: The recommended recovery method from an overpitching rotor is:
questions.explanation: The recommended recovery method from an overpitching rotor is to lower the collective smoothly and simultaneously open the thottle to restore rotor RPM. The reduces the pitch on the blades whilst maintaining correct R/RPM.

questions.question: Rotor thrust is:
options: perpendicular to the rotation on the disc
always in line with the mast
questions.explanation: Rotor thrust is the force generated by the rotor system that acts perpendicular to the plane of rotation of the rotor disc.

questions.question: Disc loading is calculated by:
options: weight multipled by the speed of the disc
weight divided by the speed of the disc
operating weight multipled by the disc area
operating weight divided by the disc area

questions.question: Airflow reversal is most likely to occur:
options: towards the tip of the retreating blade
towards the tip of the advancing blade
towards the rotor hub of the retreating blade
towards the rotor hub of the advancing blade

questions.question: What is the force load factor on a helicopter in a 30° angle of bank?
questions.explanation:
At a 30° angle of bank, the load factor on a helicopter is 1.2 Gs. The load factor (G-force) in a banked turn is calculated using:
Load Factor
=
1
cos
⁡
(
bank angle
)
Load Factor= 
cos(bank angle)
1
​

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the three regions of a rotor blade in autorotation?
options: Driven region, driving region, stall region
Advancing region, retreating region, feathering region
Forward region, trailing region, yawing region
Translational region, vertical region, coning region

questions.question: In the driving region of a rotor blade during autorotation, what is the direction of the aerodynamic force relative to the blade’s motion?
options: It acts slightly forward of the axis of rotation, contributing to rotor speed
It opposes rotation and reduces blade RPM
It acts downward through the disc, aiding lift
questions.explanation: In the driving region, the aerodynamic force is directed in the same direction as the blade's rotation, producing torque to sustain rotor RPM during autorotation.

questions.question: What happens in the driven region of a blade during autorotation?
options: The blade produces thrust to maintain climb
Total aerodynamic force acts to accelerate the rotor
Drag exceeds lift, resulting in a decelerating effect on the rotor
questions.explanation: In the driven region of a rotor blade during autorotation, the aerodynamic force acts against the direction of rotation, causing the blades to consume power rather than produce it.

questions.question: Best endurance speed typically corresponds to which point on the helicopter’s power required curve?
options: The lowest point on the curve
The point where drag equals thrust
The steepest part of the curve
Just above the speed for retreating blade stall

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of flying at best endurance speed in a helicopter?
options: To maintain translational lift during cruise
To reach the destination in the shortest possible time
To achieve maximum range at the lowest fuel burn
To maximise time airborne for a given amount of fuel
questions.explanation: Best endurance speed is the speed at which the helicopter consumes the least fuel per unit of time, allowing it to stay airborne for the longest possible duration with a given amount of fuel. This is particularly important for flights that require loitering or extended time on station, such as search and rescue or surveillance operations.

questions.question: Best endurance speed is generally:
options: Higher than the speed for minimum drag
Equal to the maximum level flight speed
The same as Vy (best rate of climb)
questions.explanation:
Best endurance speed is the speed at which the aircraft consumes the least power (fuel flow) per unit time, maximising time aloft. This speed is usually lower, because flying slower reduces power required (especially near the bottom of the power curve).
Best range speed is the speed at which the aircraft achieves the maximum distance per unit of fuel (fuel efficiency). This speed is typically higher than best endurance speed because flying a bit faster reduces the total time for a given distance, even if power consumption is slightly higher.

questions.question: What is rotor wash recirculation most likely to cause during a hover in confined areas?
options: Uncommanded yaw due to tail rotor interference
Decreased rotor efficiency and loss of lift
Increased translational lift and climb performance

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the airflow pattern during recirculation?
options: Exhaust air from the tail rotor disrupts forward airflow
The downwash strikes the ground and is drawn back up into the rotor disc
Airflow enters from above the disc and is expelled laterally
Air spirals upward around the tail boom due to torque

questions.question: What performance indication might suggest the helicopter is experiencing recirculation?
options: Decreasing power required and increasing climb rate
Increasing power required with little or no altitude gain
Sudden reduction in tail rotor authority

questions.question: Which flight condition increases the likelihood of compressibility effects on a rotor system?
options: Low gross weight and low density altitude
High altitude, slow speed, and low blade RPM
High forward speed and high rotor RPM
Hovering in ground effect on a calm day

questions.question: What is a common symptom of compressibility effects at the rotor blade tips?
options: Reduced torque and smoother control response
Onset of blade stall on the retreating side
Vibration and control feedback due to shock formation
questions.explanation: Compressibility effects at rotor tips typically cause vibrations, buffeting, and control difficulties due to shock wave formation and blade tip stall.

questions.question: Where on the rotor disc are compressibility effects most likely to first appear?
options: Near the rotor hub due to coning
On the retreating blade due to reverse flow
At the advancing blade tip due to high relative airspeed
At the trailing edge of all blades equally
questions.explanation: Compressibility effects on a helicopter rotor disc are most likely to first appear at the tip of the advancing blade. This is because the advancing blade tip combines the highest rotational speed with the forward airspeed of the helicopter.

questions.question: What happens to the coning angle when overpitching?
questions.explanation: Overpitching leads to an increased coning angle due to higher lift and potentially reduced rotor RPM, compromising rotor stability and efficiency.

questions.question: As air flows through a venturi constriction, static pressure ______________ and as velocity__________________
questions.explanation: According to Bernoulli’s principle, in a flowing fluid when velocity increases (like in the narrow throat of a venturi), the pressure decreases. So, at the venturi throat (the narrowest part) the airspeed is highest and static pressure is lowest.
questions.kd: Unit 1.3.1.  2.2.1 Apply Bernoulli's theorem of constant energy flow to describe how an aerofoil produces lift, limited to the variation of kinetic energy (dynamic pressure) and potential energy (static pressure) as air flows through a venturi or over an aerofoil

questions.question: Best range speed in still air occurs at which point on the helicopter’s power required curve?
options: At the point of minimum power required
At the point of maximum torque
Where the tangent from the origin meets the curve
Where total drag exceeds induced drag

questions.question: What is the typical relationship between best range and best endurance speeds?
options: Best range speed is typically lower than best endurance speed
They are always identical in calm conditions
Best range speed is typically higher than best endurance speed
Best range speed only applies in autorotation
questions.explanation:
Best endurance speed: The speed at which the aircraft uses the least fuel per unit of time, maximizing time aloft. Best range speed: The speed that provides the greatest distance per unit of fuel consumed, maximizing distance travelled per fuel unit. Best endurance = slower speed, maximising time in the air.
Best range = faster speed than endurance, maximising distance flown per fuel unit.

questions.question: What does flying at best range speed optimise?
options: Time aloft for a given fuel load

questions.question: What drag component decreases when relative airspeed is increased?
questions.explanation: As relative airspeed increases, induced drag decreases, but parasite drag increases, leading to a total drag curve with a minimum drag point at an optimal speed (best lift-to-drag ratio).

questions.question: In the diagram of the aerofoil, label A corresponds to which of the following?
In the diagram of the aerofoil, label B corresponds to which of the following?
In the diagram of the aerofoil, label C corresponds to which of the following?
In the diagram of the aerofoil, label D corresponds to which of the following?
In the diagram of the aerofoil, label E corresponds to which of the following?
In the diagram of the aerofoil, label F corresponds to which of the following?
In the diagram of the aerofoil, label G corresponds to which of the following?
In the diagram of the aerofoil, label H corresponds to which of the following?
In the diagram of the aerofoil, label I corresponds to which of the following?
In the diagram of the aerofoil, label J corresponds to which of the following?
In the diagram of the aerofoil, label K corresponds to which of the following?
In the diagram of the aerofoil, label L corresponds to which of the following?
What is the main function of a horizontal stabiliser on a helicopter during forward flight?
options: To produce lift to support the tail
To provide pitch stability by producing a downward force

questions.question: In cruise, what aerodynamic force is the horizontal stabiliser primarily countering?
options: Nose-down moment from the rotor thrust vector

questions.question: Why is a negative camber on the horizontal stabiliser commonly found on helicopters?
options: to produce a downward force on the tail plane during forward flight
to counteract the positive camber on the main rotor blades
to counteract the positive camber on the tail rotor blades
questions.explanation: A negative camber on the horizontal stabilizer of a helicopter is commonly used to produce downward aerodynamic force (negative lift), which helps to counteract the nose-up pitching moment generated by the main rotor.
questions.kd: Unit 1.3.1   2.1.1(a) aerofoil span, chord, camber, thickness/chord ratio

questions.question: What will be the effect on the rate of descent during an autorotation at a constant speed if the collective is raised and the RRPM reduces to the minimum limit?
A main rotor blade out of track will produce:
questions.explanation: A main rotor blade out of track will produce low frequency vertical vibrations.

questions.question: In a hovering flight, tail rotor drift would result, if uncorrected, in the helicopter:
questions.explanation: In hovering flight, tail rotor drift (also known as translating tendency) would result, if uncorrected, in the helicopter drifting laterally to the right.

questions.question: To recover from vortex ring state, the pilot should
options: Increase collective and increase rotor RPM
Decrease collective and increase rotor RPM
questions.explanation: To recover from vortex ring state, the pilot should decrease collective to reduce the angle of attack and increase airspeed to move out of the disturbed airflow. This helps to restore normal airflow through the rotor disc and regain lift.
questions.kd: Unit 1.3.3.  2.2.3(a) vortex ring state (settling with power)

questions.question: The aerodynamic flapping of a fully-articulated main rotor system in forward flight causes the advancing blade to
Transverse flow effect causes :
options: Lift and downwash velocity to be constant over all portions of the rotor disc
Lift over the rear of the disc to be less than lift at the front
The downwash velocity to be less at the rear of the disc than at the front
Lift at the front of the rotor disc to be less than at the rear
questions.explanation: During traverse flow effect, the air flows more efficiently through the front of the rotor disc (clean, undisturbed air). The rear of the disc is still in vertical, disturbed air which creates a difference in lift between the front and rear halves of the rotor disc more lift at the front and less lift at the rear.

questions.question: The condition where the drag of the main rotor blades is so great that the engine cannot maintain rotor RPM is known as:
questions.explanation:
Overpitching occurs when the collective is raised excessively, increasing the angle of attack on the rotor blades.
This increases blade drag significantly to a point where the engine cannot supply enough power to overcome that drag rotor RPM begins to decay and lift production drops.

questions.question: A helicopter is autorotating from a high to low density altitude with collective pitch at minimum setting and airspeed constant. Which statement which correctly describes the variation of rotor RPM and rate of descent:
options: Rotor RPM will decrease and the ROD will increase
Both rotor RPM and ROD will increase
Both rotor RPM and ROD will increase
Rotor RPM will increase and ROD will increase
questions.explanation: During autorotation, as the helicopter descends from a high to low density altitude, the rotor RPM will decrease due to the reduced air density providing less aerodynamic force on the blades. Simultaneously, the rate of descent (ROD) will increase because the rotor is generating less lift in the thinner air, causing the helicopter to descend faster.

questions.question: Torque effect of the main rotor will tend to:
options: Roll the helicopter to the right
Rotate the fuselage to the right
Rotate the fuselage to the left
Roll the helicopter to the left
questions.explanation: The torque effect of the main rotor will tend to rotate the fuselage in the opposite direction of the main rotor blades. For a main rotor that rotates clockwise (as viewed from above), the torque effect will cause the fuselage to rotate to the right.

questions.question: In a steady hover in nil wind conditions, the direction of the relative airflow will be
options: Downwards through the plane of rotation at an angle greater than the pitch angle
Upwards through the plane of rotation at an angle less than the pitch angle
Downwards through the plane of rotation at an angle less than the pitch angle
Upwards through the plane of rotation at an angle greater than the pitch angle
questions.explanation: In a steady hover in nil wind conditions, the relative airflow will be downwards through the plane of rotation at an angle less than the pitch angle. This is because the rotor blades are generating lift by accelerating air directly downwards, creating a downward flow of air through the rotor disc.

questions.question: What is the definition of rotor thrust?
options: The force acting perpendicular to relative airflow opposite to rotor drag
The force acting perpendicular to the plane of rotation opposite to torque
The force acting parallel to the plane
The force acting perpendicular to the plane of rotation directly opposite to weight
questions.explanation: Rotor thrust is the force acting perpendicular to the plane of rotation directly opposite to weight.

questions.question: During a still air hover left pedal is applied:
options: Tail rotor thrust will increase and the helicopter will sink
Tail rotor thrust will decrease and the helicopter will sink
Tail rotor thrust will increase and the helicopter will climb
Tail rotor thrust will decrease and the helicopter will climb
questions.explanation: During a hover in still air, applying left pedal will cause the helicopter to yaw (rotate) left.

questions.question: If uncorrected, tail rotor drift will cause the helicopter to:
options: Move sideways towards the advancing blade
Move sideways towards the retreating blade
questions.explanation: If uncorrected, tail rotor drift will cause the helicopter to drift laterally (usually to the right), due to the side thrust of the tail rotor.

questions.question: The conditions most likely to induce “settling with power":
options: Steep ascent with high power settings
Steep descent at high power settings
In forward light at speeds above velocity never exceed speed
questions.explanation: Settling with power occurs when a helicopter is flying at a low forward speed and high descent rate, typically in a steep descent with high power settings.
questions.kd: Unit 1.3.3.  2.2.3(a) vortex ring state (settling with power)

questions.question: A rigid rotor system has the freedom to
options: Flap, but not feather or drag
Feather, but not flap or drag
Flap and feather, but not drag
questions.explanation: A rigid rotor system has the freedom to feather, but not flap or drag. Examples of helicopter types with rigid rotor systems include the Bell 407 and the Eurocopter EC135.

questions.question: In forward flight the angle of attack will be highest:
options: Abeam the helicopter on the retreating side
Abeam the helicopter on the advancing side
questions.explanation: In forward flight, the angle of attack will be highest abeam the helicopter on the retreating side. This is because the relative airflow is slower on the retreating side due to the combination of forward speed and rotor rotation, resulting in a higher angle of attack compared to the advancing side.

questions.question: A helicopter is autorotating at the minimum Rate of Descent air speed. What effect will a decrease in Indicated Airspeed have:
questions.explanation: A decrease in airspeed below minimum rate of descent speed will cause the helicopter to descend more rapidly, this will also increase the R/RPM.
